content:
30 min read
Just now

--

Between the corporations optimizing us out of the org chart and the governments optimizing us out of the decision, there is a third path: bound the machine, and let institutions be human.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Machine Must Stop — Image by adflegal.orgThe Boundary Moves
In Neither Gods Nor Martyrs, I argued that the great debates over medical technology had been captured by two camps having the wrong conversation. On one side stood the transhumanists, who see the human body as legacy hardware awaiting an upgrade. On the other stood the bioconservatives, newly armed with Pope Leo XIV’s May 2026 encyclical Magnifica Humanitas, who warn that a civilization drunk on optimization will quietly decide that some lives are less worth keeping. Against both, I proposed a third position — call it therapeutic humanism, or the healing mean — built on a single load-bearing distinction: the difference between restoration and optimization. Restoration is bounded. It aims at a baseline, at returning a person to the full health proper to a human being, and it carries its own stopping point in its definition. You cannot over-cure malaria. Optimization is unbounded. It aims at a maximum, and a maximum has no natural ceiling; a project with no stopping point will, given enough power, keep going until it has remade everything, including the people it was supposed to serve.
That essay was about bodies. This one is not, because the most consequential application of the healing mean in the twenty-first century is not medical at all.
The optimization logic that the encyclical diagnosed does not live primarily in gene-editing labs or longevity clinics. It lives in the org chart, in the data center, and in the national-security state. It lives in the corporation that dreams of the near-zero-human company, restructuring itself at machine speed to maximize output while treating its own employees as friction. It lives in the individual professional who, offered a machine that thinks faster than he does, quietly stops thinking at all. And it lives in a government that has begun treating advanced software as a weapon — a thing to be gated, vetted, export-controlled, and, when the politics demand it, switched off globally overnight. Each of these is the same move the encyclical warned against, performed on a different patient. The person, the firm, and the state are each being reframed as systems to be maximized rather than institutions to be kept healthy, and in each case the unmaximized element — which is to say, the human element — starts to look like a liability on the balance sheet.
The public conversation about this, like the medical one before it, has collapsed into a false binary. One camp insists that we must accelerate without reservation, that every constraint on algorithmic capital or strategic AI is a tax paid to timidity, and that the humans displaced along the way are the necessary cost of progress. The other camp counsels rejection: refuse the tools, halt the data centers, freeze the models. Gods or martyrs, again. And again, both camps are answering a question that most people are not asking. Almost nobody wants an economy run by autonomous corporate software or a security state with a kill switch over the world’s intelligence infrastructure. Almost nobody wants to smash the machines, either. What people want — when you ask them directly — is that the technology make their work more human rather than less, their towns more prosperous rather than more extracted, and their governments more accountable rather than more opaque. That is the healing mean, applied beyond the body. It has simply lacked a name and a defense in this territory, because here too the megaphones have been held by the people arguing about gods.
This essay is an attempt to supply that defense. It proceeds through three concentric circles — the individual, the corporation, and the state — because the optimization logic operates at all three scales and the failures compound across them. A workforce whose judgment has atrophied cannot check a corporation that has automated its own governance, and a corporate sector organized around pure extraction cannot resist a state that has learned it can seize the switch. At each scale, I will apply the same diagnostic filter developed in the earlier essay, and at each scale I will try to show that the choice we actually face is not between acceleration and refusal, but between bounded restoration and unbounded optimization.
The Filter, Restated
Three criteria organize everything that follows, and each descends directly from the argument of Neither Gods Nor Martyrs and from the encyclical that anchored it.
The first is ontological dignity. Human worth is inherent and existential. It is, in Leo’s phrasing, “neither acquired nor earned,” and it “does not need to be justified” by output, efficiency, or utility. This is not an argument against performance review or compensation tied to output — those are legitimate management tools. The claim is narrower: an employee’s fitness for a role is a separate question from an employee’s worth as a person, and the two must not collapse into one number. A metric can say whether a job is being done well. It cannot say how much basic consideration a person is owed. The moment an institution lets the first quietly answer the second, low scorers stop looking merely reassignable and start looking dispensable — and a system that has erased the distinction will eventually act on it.
The second is finitude as foundation. Human limits — bounded attention, the need for rest, vulnerability, the fact that a person can hold only so much and decide only so fast — are not bugs to be automated away. They are the ground on which meaning, relationship, and institutional trust are built. The encyclical’s most quoted line, that “humanity flourishes not despite limitations, but often through them,” applies to organizations as much as to bodies. An institution that engineers away every human limit does not become a better institution. It becomes something else, and the something else has no particular reason to care about the creatures it was built to serve.
The third is restoration of the self. Technology passes the filter when it returns human beings to full agency — when it clears away the drudgery, the noise, and the curable dysfunction so that judgment, creativity, and responsibility can operate. It fails the filter when it replaces agency, when the human is reduced to approving what the machine has already decided, or removed from the loop entirely. Restoration preserves the agent. Substitution dissolves them. These are not the same act, and the whole argument turns on refusing to let them blur.
With the filter in hand, begin at the smallest scale: the single working mind.
Part One: The Individual — The Value of the Unassisted Mind
The Inversion of Professional Value
For sixty years, the knowledge economy ran on a simple bargain that Peter Drucker first described in 1959 when he coined the term “knowledge worker”: prove your worth by doing. Write the cleanest code, build the most accurate model, draft the most airtight contract, and the hierarchy would reward you. Execution was the proof of expertise, and the entire scaffolding of professional life — the universities, the case competitions, the junior-associate years, the career ladder itself — was built to produce and reward excellent executors.
That bargain is not so much being dissolved as rewritten, and the rewriting is happening faster than most job descriptions can keep up with. The exposure is concentrated in the white-collar heart of the knowledge economy — Goldman Sachs estimated in 2023 that roughly two-thirds of jobs in the United States and Europe carry some degree of automation exposure, concentrated in office administration, law, and other credentialed professions rather than in factories — but exposure to automation is not the same claim as disappearance of the job. What the more recent data actually shows is a shift in what a given role requires: the McKinsey Global Institute’s 2025 estimate of technically automatable work hours nearly doubled its own 2023 projection in the space of two years, and the World Economic Forum expects close to forty percent of the skills employers need to change by 2030. The honest reading of numbers moving that fast is not that the jobs are vanishing on a predictable schedule. It is that nobody — not the economists, not the employers, not the workers — can currently say with confidence what a given profession will require of the people in it five years out, and that uncertainty is itself the condition knowledge workers now have to operate inside.
In The Architect’s Inversion, I described what this does to the hierarchy of professional value. Execution — the procedural fluency that took a decade to build and that once sat at the top of the pyramid — becomes a commodity, produced on demand by what we might call System Zero: an external cognitive circuit that processes data, recognizes patterns, generates options, and scaffolds decisions before conscious human deliberation is even engaged. What becomes scarce, and therefore valuable, is everything upstream of execution: framing the right problem, specifying what a good outcome looks like, evaluating whether the machine’s flawless output actually serves the intended purpose, and owning the consequences when it does not. The craftsperson’s economy gives way to the architect’s economy, and the primary failure mode of professional life shifts from execution error — the bug, the typo, the miscalculation — to goal misalignment: the beautifully executed solution to the wrong problem, failing invisibly and at scale.
Read through the healing mean, the inversion itself is neither good nor bad. It is a change in where human value concentrates. The moral question is what we do to the human mind in response — and here the evidence should alarm anyone.
The Risk to the Instrument
The evidence here needs to be handled carefully, because it has been overstated in both directions online. A widely circulated MIT Media Lab study found that participants drafting essays with ChatGPT showed lower EEG brain engagement than those working unaided, with weaker activation in the prefrontal cortex specifically. It is a small, preliminary, task-specific result — it shows what happened during one kind of writing exercise, not that AI use causes lasting cognitive damage, and it should not be read as more than that. But it lines up with a broader and more carefully hedged pattern in the literature: several studies of knowledge workers have found that higher confidence in an AI’s answers correlates with less effort spent evaluating them, and that heavy, passive reliance can narrow the range of solutions a group produces for the same problem. Researchers call the underlying tendency automation bias — leaning on automated systems at the expense of independent checking — compounded by the ordinary human preference for the path of least cognitive effort.
None of this means using AI erodes the mind; it means how it is used matters. The risk is concentrated in passive, substitute use, and it lands hardest on people early in a career, who risk reaching for architectural judgment without ever having built the grinding, hands-on experience that judgment is usually made from.
Applying the Filter: Assisted, Not Substituted
This is where the restoration-versus-optimization distinction stops being a philosophical nicety and becomes a design specification.
The optimizing move treats the individual mind as a bottleneck. Its metric is throughput: how much human deliberation can be removed from the process, how quickly the machine’s output can be approved and shipped. The research literature calls this substitute use — the human accepts the AI’s output as the conclusion, offloading evaluation entirely. Substitute use
